title: 3 Technical Specifications for Clearing Files
body_text:
3	Technical Specifications for Clearing Files
3.1	Overview of Technical Specifications for Clearing Files
This Chapter provides information about clearing file availability, file and transaction dating, and retrieval/posting guidelines for files.
3.2	Clearing File Availability and Null Files
Most files are available seven days a week. If there are no transactions for a specific day, the file is a ânull fileâ consisting of one A record and one Z record.
The exception applies to the UC01, UN01 and UNRT files, which are created for transactions dated Monday to Friday only and are available the same day. These files arenât created for weekends but are created on Statutory holidays that occur Monday to Friday.
Availability times for outgoing files from Central 1 are provided in the applicable file specification documents. Exceptions should be reported to Central 1, Client Support Services. Files are available for approximately 30 calendar days after the date they are first made available.

3.3	File Naming Convention
Some file names include the date the file is first made available. This date is âtodayâ and it is the date the file should be posted. For specific file naming conventions see the applicable file specifications document.
3.4	Retrieval/Posting Guidelines and Null Files
Central 1 recommends that files be retrieved and posted when they are available. If this isnât feasible, a financial institution can choose to retrieve and post a weekend or statutory holiday file on the next business day. In this case, the financial institution should post files in date order.
In most cases, null files are provided. Provision of null files means that the financial institution receives a file for every day of the year. An exception is the CR01 file, which is created for transactions dated for business days only. The CR01 file is made available prior to the posting date, but the transactions must be posted on the due date.
All other files, including null files, can be posted upon retrieval.
3.5	General Specifications of Clearing Files
Clearing files are provided in compressed file format, meaning that fields are of variable length and are delimited by punctuation. For further information, see the specific file specification document.
3.6	AFT Clearing (Electronic) File Specifications
This Section describes file format specifications for CAD and USD AFT clearing files. These files can include the following types of transactions:
Record Type
Description
C Record (Credit)
Credit transaction your financial institution is receiving.
Credit transaction your financial institution is originating.
D Record (Debit)
Debit transaction your financial institution is receiving.
Debit transaction your financial institution is originating.
E Record (Debit)
Reversal of a credit. Your financial institution is electronically reversing a transaction (originated by you or your business member) that has already been credited to your account. The reversal will be a debit to your account.
Reversal of a credit. Another financial institution is reversing a transaction (that they originated and your financial institution received) that has already been credited to your account. The reversal will be a debit to your account.
F Record (Credit)
Reversal of a debit. Your financial institution is reversing a transaction (originated by you or your business member) that has already been debited from your account. The reversal will be a credit to your account.
Reversal of a debit. Another financial institution is reversing a transaction (that they originated and you received) that has already been debited from your account. The reversal will be a credit to your account.
I Record (Credit)
Return of a direct deposit. Another financial institution is electronically returning a transaction that you originated. The original transaction was a debit to your account. The return will be a credit to your account.
Return of a pre-authorized payment. Your financial institution is electronically returning a transaction that another financial institution originated. The original transaction has already been debited from your account. The return will be a credit to your account.
Return of a reversal of a pre-authorized payment. Another financial institution is returning a reversal that you originated. The reversal was a credit to the other financial institution and a debit to your account. The return of the reversal will be a credit to your account.
Recall of a direct deposit. Your financial institution originated a transaction that was a credit to the other financial institution and a debit to your account. You then recalled the transaction. The recall will be a credit to your account.
J Record (Debit)
Return of a pre-authorized payment. Another financial institution is electronically returning a transaction that your financial institution originated. The original transaction was a credit to you. The return will be a debit to your account.
Return of a direct deposit. Your financial institution is electronically returning a transaction that another financial institution originated. The original transaction has already been credited to your account as a credit. The return will be a debit that will be posted to your account.
Return of a reversal of a direct deposit. Another financial institution is electronically returning a reversal that your financial institution originated. The reversal was a debit to the other financial institution and a credit to you. The return will be a debit to your account.
Recall of a direct deposit. Another financial institution originated a transaction that was a debit to them and a credit to your account. The financial institution then recalled the transaction. The recall will be a debit to your account.
END
